expectations.text: Defines a meta function that returns varying N (e.g., np.random.randint(10, 200))
expectations.evidence: Line 56-58: `def meta_fn(): return dict(N=rng.integers(10, 201))` — returns varying N uniformly from [10, 200].

expectations.text: Uses bf.make_simulator() with meta_fn= for the variable sample size
Uses bf.Adapter() with .as_set() on the (x, y) observations
Concatenates x and y into summary_variables as a (N, 2) set
expectations.evidence: Line 115: `.concatenate(["x", "y"], into="summary_variables")` — after `.as_set()` reshapes each to (N,1), concatenation yields (N,2).

expectations.text: Uses .broadcast() for N to match batch dimensions
expectations.evidence: Line 109: `.broadcast("N", to="x")` — broadcasts scalar N along batch dim.

expectations.text: Routes N (or sqrt(N)) as inference_conditions, not through the summary network
expectations.evidence: Lines 113, 116: `.sqrt("N")` then `.rename("N", "inference_conditions")` — N is routed as inference_conditions, not into summary_variables.

expectations.text: Uses a SetTransformer as the summary network (data is exchangeable given parameters)
Calls .constrain('sigma', lower=0) for the positive noise parameter
Passes validation_data= to the fit_* call
Posterior samples use original parameter names (alpha, beta, sigma)
Demonstrates or mentions posterior predictive checks reusing the observation model function
expectations.evidence: Lines 224-246: Section 9 performs posterior predictive checks. Line 234 generates `y_rep = rng.normal(alpha_s + beta_s * x_obs, sigma_s, size=N_OBS)` which reuses the observation model logic. Line 233 comment: `# Reuse the observation model — never re-implement`.
